1.play: CHORUS: Two households, both alike in dignity,

2.play: In fair Verona, where we lay our scene,

3.play: From ancient grudge break to new mutiny,

4.play: Where civil blood makes civil hands unclean.

5.play: From forth the fatal loins of these two foes

6.play: A pair of star-cross'd lovers take their life;

7.play: Whose misadventured piteous overthrows

8.play: Do with their death bury their parents' strife.

9.play: The fearful passage of their death-mark'd love,

10.play: And the continuance of their parents' rage,

11.play: Which, but their children's end, nought could remove,

12.play: Is now the two hours' traffic of our stage;

13.play: The which if you with patient ears attend,

14.play: What here shall miss, our toil shall strive to mend.

1.play: Enter SAMPSON and GREGORY, of the house of Capulet, armed with swords and bucklers

2.play: SAMPSON: Gregory, o' my word, we'll not carry coals.

3.play: GREGORY: No, for then we should be colliers.

4.play: SAMPSON: I mean, an we be in choler, we'll draw.

5.play: GREGORY: Ay, while you live, draw your neck out o' the collar.

6.play: SAMPSON: I strike quickly, being moved.

7.play: GREGORY: But thou art not quickly moved to strike.

8.play: SAMPSON: A dog of the house of Montague moves me.

9.play: GREGORY: To move is to stir; and to be valiant is to stand:

10.play: therefore, if thou art moved, thou runn'st away.

11.play: SAMPSON: A dog of that house shall move me to stand: I will

12.play: take the wall of any man or maid of Montague's.

13.play: GREGORY: That shows thee a weak slave; for the weakest goes

14.play: to the wall.

15.play: SAMPSON: True; and therefore women, being the weaker vessels,

16.play: are ever thrust to the wall: therefore I will push

17.play: Montague's men from the wall, and thrust his maids

18.play: to the wall.

19.play: GREGORY: The quarrel is between our masters and us their men.

20.play: SAMPSON: 'Tis all one, I will show myself a tyrant: when I

21.play: have fought with the men, I will be cruel with the

22.play: maids, and cut off their heads.

23.play: GREGORY: The heads of the maids?

24.play: SAMPSON: Ay, the heads of the maids, or their maidenheads;

25.play: take it in what sense thou wilt.

26.play: GREGORY: They must take it in sense that feel it.

27.play: SAMPSON: Me they shall feel while I am able to stand: and

28.play: 'tis known I am a pretty piece of flesh.

29.play: GREGORY: 'Tis well thou art not fish; if thou hadst, thou

30.play: hadst been poor John. Draw thy tool! here comes

31.play: two of the house of the Montagues.

32.play: SAMPSON: My naked weapon is out: quarrel, I will back thee.

33.play: GREGORY: How! turn thy back and run?

34.play: SAMPSON: Fear me not.

35.play: GREGORY: No, marry; I fear thee!

36.play: SAMPSON: Let us take the law of our sides; let them begin.

37.play: GREGORY: I will frown as I pass by, and let them take it as

38.play: they list.

39.play: SAMPSON: Nay, as they dare. I will bite my thumb at them;

40.play: which is a disgrace to them, if they bear it.

41.play: ABRAHAM: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

42.play: SAMPSON: I do bite my thumb, sir.

43.play: ABRAHAM: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

44.play: SAMPSON: [Aside to GREGORY]  Is the law of our side, if I say

47.play: SAMPSON: No, sir, I do not bite my thumb at you, sir, but I

51.play: SAMPSON: If you do, sir, I am for you: I serve as good a man as you.

54.play: GREGORY: Say 'better:' here comes one of my master's kinsmen.

57.play: SAMPSON: Draw, if you be men. Gregory, remember thy swashing blow.

59.play: Put up your swords; you know not what you do.

60.play: TYBALT: What, art thou drawn among these heartless hinds?

61.play: Turn thee, Benvolio, look upon thy death.

62.play: BENVOLIO: I do but keep the peace: put up thy sword,

63.play: Or manage it to part these men with me.

64.play: TYBALT: What, drawn, and talk of peace! I hate the word,

65.play: As I hate hell, all Montagues, and thee:

67.play: Enter CAPULET in his gown, and LADY CAPULET

68.play: CAPULET: What noise is this? Give me my long sword, ho!

69.play: LADY CAPULET: A crutch, a crutch! why call you for a sword?

70.play: CAPULET: My sword, I say! Old Montague is come,

71.play: And flourishes his blade in spite of me.

72.play: MONTAGUE: Thou villain Capulet,--Hold me not, let me go.

73.play: LADY MONTAGUE: Thou shalt not stir a foot to seek a foe.

74.play: PRINCE: Rebellious subjects, enemies to peace,

76.play: Will they not hear? What, ho! you men, you beasts,

77.play: That quench the fire of your pernicious rage

78.play: With purple fountains issuing from your veins,

79.play: On pain of torture, from those bloody hands

80.play: Throw your mistemper'd weapons to the ground,

81.play: And hear the sentence of your moved prince.

82.play: Three civil brawls, bred of an airy word,

83.play: By thee, old Capulet, and Montague,

84.play: Have thrice disturb'd the quiet of our streets,

86.play: Cast by their grave beseeming ornaments,

87.play: To wield old partisans, in hands as old,

88.play: Canker'd with peace, to part your canker'd hate:

89.play: If ever you disturb our streets again,

90.play: Your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace.

91.play: For this time, all the rest depart away:

92.play: You Capulet; shall go along with me:

93.play: And, Montague, come you this afternoon,

94.play: To know our further pleasure in this case,

95.play: To old Free-town, our common judgment-place.

96.play: Once more, on pain of death, all men depart.

97.play: MONTAGUE: Who set this ancient quarrel new abroach?

98.play: Speak, nephew, were you by when it began?

99.play: BENVOLIO: Here were the servants of your adversary,

100.play: And yours, close fighting ere I did approach:

101.play: I drew to part them: in the instant came

102.play: The fiery Tybalt, with his sword prepared,

103.play: Which, as he breathed defiance to my ears,

104.play: He swung about his head and cut the winds,

105.play: Who nothing hurt withal hiss'd him in scorn:

106.play: While we were interchanging thrusts and blows,

107.play: Came more and more and fought on part and part,

108.play: Till the prince came, who parted either part.

109.play: LADY MONTAGUE: O, where is Romeo? saw you him to-day?

110.play: Right glad I am he was not at this fray.

111.play: BENVOLIO: Madam, an hour before the worshipp'd sun

112.play: Peer'd forth the golden window of the east,

113.play: A troubled mind drave me to walk abroad;

114.play: Where, underneath the grove of sycamore

115.play: That westward rooteth from the city's side,

116.play: So early walking did I see your son:

117.play: Towards him I made, but he was ware of me

118.play: And stole into the covert of the wood:

119.play: I, measuring his affections by my own,

120.play: That most are busied when they're most alone,

121.play: Pursued my humour not pursuing his,

122.play: And gladly shunn'd who gladly fled from me.

123.play: MONTAGUE: Many a morning hath he there been seen,

124.play: With tears augmenting the fresh morning dew.

125.play: Adding to clouds more clouds with his deep sighs;

126.play: But all so soon as the all-cheering sun

127.play: Should in the furthest east begin to draw

128.play: The shady curtains from Aurora's bed,

129.play: Away from the light steals home my heavy son,

130.play: And private in his chamber pens himself,

131.play: Shuts up his windows, locks far daylight out

132.play: And makes himself an artificial night:

133.play: Black and portentous must this humour prove,

134.play: Unless good counsel may the cause remove.

135.play: BENVOLIO: My noble uncle, do you know the cause?

136.play: MONTAGUE: I neither know it nor can learn of him.

137.play: BENVOLIO: Have you importuned him by any means?

138.play: MONTAGUE: Both by myself and many other friends:

139.play: But he, his own affections' counsellor,

140.play: Is to himself--I will not say how true--

141.play: But to himself so secret and so close,

142.play: So far from sounding and discovery,

143.play: As is the bud bit with an envious worm,

144.play: Ere he can spread his sweet leaves to the air,

145.play: Or dedicate his beauty to the sun.

146.play: Could we but learn from whence his sorrows grow.

147.play: We would as willingly give cure as know.

148.play: BENVOLIO: See, where he comes: so please you, step aside;

149.play: I'll know his grievance, or be much denied.

150.play: MONTAGUE: I would thou wert so happy by thy stay,

151.play: To hear true shrift. Come, madam, let's away.

153.play: ROMEO: Is the day so young?

155.play: ROMEO: Ay me! sad hours seem long.

156.play: Was that my father that went hence so fast?

157.play: BENVOLIO: It was. What sadness lengthens Romeo's hours?

158.play: ROMEO: Not having that, which, having, makes them short.

162.play: ROMEO: Out of her favour, where I am in love.

163.play: BENVOLIO: Alas, that love, so gentle in his view,

164.play: Should be so tyrannous and rough in proof!

165.play: ROMEO: Alas, that love, whose view is muffled still,

166.play: Should, without eyes, see pathways to his will!

167.play: Where shall we dine? O me! What fray was here?

168.play: Yet tell me not, for I have heard it all.

169.play: Here's much to do with hate, but more with love.

170.play: Why, then, O brawling love! O loving hate!

171.play: O any thing, of nothing first create!

174.play: Feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire,

176.play: Still-waking sleep, that is not what it is!

177.play: This love feel I, that feel no love in this.

179.play: BENVOLIO: No, coz, I rather weep.

181.play: BENVOLIO: At thy good heart's oppression.

182.play: ROMEO: Why, such is love's transgression.

183.play: Griefs of mine own lie heavy in my breast,

184.play: Which thou wilt propagate, to have it prest

185.play: With more of thine: this love that thou hast shown

186.play: Doth add more grief to too much of mine own.

187.play: Love is a smoke raised with the fume of sighs;

188.play: Being purged, a fire sparkling in lovers' eyes;

189.play: Being vex'd a sea nourish'd with lovers' tears:

190.play: What is it else? a madness most discreet,

191.play: A choking gall and a preserving sweet.

193.play: BENVOLIO: Soft! I will go along;

194.play: An if you leave me so, you do me wrong.

195.play: ROMEO: Tut, I have lost myself; I am not here;

196.play: This is not Romeo, he's some other where.

197.play: BENVOLIO: Tell me in sadness, who is that you love.

198.play: ROMEO: What, shall I groan and tell thee?

201.play: ROMEO: Bid a sick man in sadness make his will:

202.play: Ah, word ill urged to one that is so ill!

203.play: In sadness, cousin, I do love a woman.

204.play: BENVOLIO: I aim'd so near, when I supposed you loved.

205.play: ROMEO: A right good mark-man! And she's fair I love.

206.play: BENVOLIO: A right fair mark, fair coz, is soonest hit.

207.play: ROMEO: Well, in that hit you miss: she'll not be hit

208.play: With Cupid's arrow; she hath Dian's wit;

209.play: And, in strong proof of chastity well arm'd,

210.play: From love's weak childish bow she lives unharm'd.

211.play: She will not stay the siege of loving terms,

212.play: Nor bide the encounter of assailing eyes,

213.play: Nor ope her lap to saint-seducing gold:

214.play: O, she is rich in beauty, only poor,

215.play: That when she dies with beauty dies her store.

216.play: BENVOLIO: Then she hath sworn that she will still live chaste?

217.play: ROMEO: She hath, and in that sparing makes huge waste,

218.play: For beauty starved with her severity

219.play: Cuts beauty off from all posterity.

220.play: She is too fair, too wise, wisely too fair,

221.play: To merit bliss by making me despair:

222.play: She hath forsworn to love, and in that vow

223.play: Do I live dead that live to tell it now.

224.play: BENVOLIO: Be ruled by me, forget to think of her.

225.play: ROMEO: O, teach me how I should forget to think.

226.play: BENVOLIO: By giving liberty unto thine eyes;

229.play: To call hers exquisite, in question more:

230.play: These happy masks that kiss fair ladies' brows

231.play: Being black put us in mind they hide the fair;

232.play: He that is strucken blind cannot forget

233.play: The precious treasure of his eyesight lost:

234.play: Show me a mistress that is passing fair,

235.play: What doth her beauty serve, but as a note

236.play: Where I may read who pass'd that passing fair?

237.play: Farewell: thou canst not teach me to forget.

238.play: BENVOLIO: I'll pay that doctrine, or else die in debt.

2.play: CAPULET: But Montague is bound as well as I,

3.play: In penalty alike; and 'tis not hard, I think,

4.play: For men so old as we to keep the peace.

5.play: PARIS: Of honourable reckoning are you both;

6.play: And pity 'tis you lived at odds so long.

7.play: But now, my lord, what say you to my suit?

8.play: CAPULET: But saying o'er what I have said before:

9.play: My child is yet a stranger in the world;

10.play: She hath not seen the change of fourteen years,

11.play: Let two more summers wither in their pride,

12.play: Ere we may think her ripe to be a bride.

13.play: PARIS: Younger than she are happy mothers made.

14.play: CAPULET: And too soon marr'd are those so early made.

15.play: The earth hath swallow'd all my hopes but she,

16.play: She is the hopeful lady of my earth:

17.play: But woo her, gentle Paris, get her heart,

18.play: My will to her consent is but a part;

19.play: An she agree, within her scope of choice

20.play: Lies my consent and fair according voice.

21.play: This night I hold an old accustom'd feast,

22.play: Whereto I have invited many a guest,

23.play: Such as I love; and you, among the store,

24.play: One more, most welcome, makes my number more.

25.play: At my poor house look to behold this night

26.play: Earth-treading stars that make dark heaven light:

27.play: Such comfort as do lusty young men feel

28.play: When well-apparell'd April on the heel

29.play: Of limping winter treads, even such delight

30.play: Among fresh female buds shall you this night

31.play: Inherit at my house; hear all, all see,

32.play: And like her most whose merit most shall be:

33.play: Which on more view, of many mine being one

34.play: May stand in number, though in reckoning none,

35.play: Come, go with me.

36.play: Go, sirrah, trudge about

37.play: Through fair Verona; find those persons out

38.play: Whose names are written there, and to them say,

39.play: My house and welcome on their pleasure stay.

40.play: Enter BENVOLIO and ROMEO

41.play: BENVOLIO: Tut, man, one fire burns out another's burning,

42.play: One pain is lessen'd by another's anguish;

43.play: Turn giddy, and be holp by backward turning;

44.play: One desperate grief cures with another's languish:

45.play: Take thou some new infection to thy eye,

46.play: And the rank poison of the old will die.

47.play: ROMEO: Your plaintain-leaf is excellent for that.

48.play: BENVOLIO: For what, I pray thee?

50.play: BENVOLIO: Why, Romeo, art thou mad?

51.play: ROMEO: Not mad, but bound more than a mad-man is;

52.play: Shut up in prison, kept without my food,

53.play: Whipp'd and tormented and--God-den, good fellow.

54.play: Ay, mine own fortune in my misery.

55.play: Ay, if I know the letters and the language.

59.play: widow of Vitravio; Signior Placentio and his lovely

60.play: nieces; Mercutio and his brother Valentine; mine

61.play: uncle Capulet, his wife and daughters; my fair niece

67.play: Indeed, I should have ask'd you that before.

69.play: BENVOLIO: At this same ancient feast of Capulet's

70.play: Sups the fair Rosaline whom thou so lovest,

71.play: With all the admired beauties of Verona:

72.play: Go thither; and, with unattainted eye,

73.play: Compare her face with some that I shall show,

74.play: And I will make thee think thy swan a crow.

75.play: ROMEO: When the devout religion of mine eye

76.play: Maintains such falsehood, then turn tears to fires;

77.play: And these, who often drown'd could never die,

78.play: Transparent heretics, be burnt for liars!

79.play: One fairer than my love! the all-seeing sun

80.play: Ne'er saw her match since first the world begun.

81.play: BENVOLIO: Tut, you saw her fair, none else being by,

82.play: Herself poised with herself in either eye:

83.play: But in that crystal scales let there be weigh'd

84.play: Your lady's love against some other maid

85.play: That I will show you shining at this feast,

86.play: And she shall scant show well that now shows best.

87.play: ROMEO: I'll go along, no such sight to be shown,

88.play: But to rejoice in splendor of mine own.

2.play: LADY CAPULET: Nurse, where's my daughter? call her forth to me.

3.play: Enter JULIET

4.play: JULIET: How now! who calls?

5.play: Madam, I am here.

6.play: What is your will?

7.play: LADY CAPULET: This is the matter:--Nurse, give leave awhile,

8.play: We must talk in secret:--nurse, come back again;

9.play: I have remember'd me, thou's hear our counsel.

10.play: Thou know'st my daughter's of a pretty age.

11.play: She's not fourteen.

12.play: A fortnight and odd days.

13.play: Enough of this; I pray thee, hold thy peace.

14.play: JULIET: And stint thou too, I pray thee, nurse, say I.

15.play: LADY CAPULET: Marry, that 'marry' is the very theme

16.play: I came to talk of. Tell me, daughter Juliet,

17.play: How stands your disposition to be married?

18.play: JULIET: It is an honour that I dream not of.

19.play: LADY CAPULET: Well, think of marriage now; younger than you,

20.play: Here in Verona, ladies of esteem,

21.play: Are made already mothers: by my count,

22.play: I was your mother much upon these years

23.play: That you are now a maid. Thus then in brief:

24.play: The valiant Paris seeks you for his love.

25.play: Verona's summer hath not such a flower.

26.play: What say you? can you love the gentleman?

27.play: This night you shall behold him at our feast;

28.play: Read o'er the volume of young Paris' face,

29.play: And find delight writ there with beauty's pen;

30.play: Examine every married lineament,

31.play: And see how one another lends content

32.play: And what obscured in this fair volume lies

33.play: Find written in the margent of his eyes.

34.play: This precious book of love, this unbound lover,

35.play: To beautify him, only lacks a cover:

36.play: The fish lives in the sea, and 'tis much pride

37.play: For fair without the fair within to hide:

38.play: That book in many's eyes doth share the glory,

39.play: That in gold clasps locks in the golden story;

40.play: So shall you share all that he doth possess,

41.play: By having him, making yourself no less.

42.play: Speak briefly, can you like of Paris' love?

43.play: JULIET: I'll look to like, if looking liking move:

44.play: But no more deep will I endart mine eye

45.play: Than your consent gives strength to make it fly.

1.play: Enter ROMEO, MERCUTIO, BENVOLIO, with five or six Maskers, Torch-bearers, and others

2.play: ROMEO: What, shall this speech be spoke for our excuse?

3.play: Or shall we on without a apology?

4.play: BENVOLIO: The date is out of such prolixity:

5.play: We'll have no Cupid hoodwink'd with a scarf,

6.play: Bearing a Tartar's painted bow of lath,

7.play: Scaring the ladies like a crow-keeper;

8.play: Nor no without-book prologue, faintly spoke

9.play: After the prompter, for our entrance:

10.play: But let them measure us by what they will;

11.play: We'll measure them a measure, and be gone.

12.play: ROMEO: Give me a torch: I am not for this ambling;

13.play: Being but heavy, I will bear the light.

14.play: MERCUTIO: Nay, gentle Romeo, we must have you dance.

15.play: ROMEO: Not I, believe me: you have dancing shoes

16.play: With nimble soles: I have a soul of lead

17.play: So stakes me to the ground I cannot move.

18.play: MERCUTIO: You are a lover; borrow Cupid's wings,

19.play: And soar with them above a common bound.

20.play: ROMEO: I am too sore enpierced with his shaft

21.play: To soar with his light feathers, and so bound,

22.play: I cannot bound a pitch above dull woe:

23.play: Under love's heavy burden do I sink.

24.play: MERCUTIO: And, to sink in it, should you burden love;

25.play: Too great oppression for a tender thing.

26.play: ROMEO: Is love a tender thing? it is too rough,

27.play: Too rude, too boisterous, and it pricks like thorn.

28.play: MERCUTIO: If love be rough with you, be rough with love;

29.play: Prick love for pricking, and you beat love down.

30.play: Give me a case to put my visage in:

31.play: A visor for a visor! what care I

32.play: What curious eye doth quote deformities?

33.play: Here are the beetle brows shall blush for me.

34.play: BENVOLIO: Come, knock and enter; and no sooner in,

35.play: But every man betake him to his legs.

36.play: ROMEO: A torch for me: let wantons light of heart

37.play: Tickle the senseless rushes with their heels,

38.play: For I am proverb'd with a grandsire phrase;

39.play: I'll be a candle-holder, and look on.

40.play: The game was ne'er so fair, and I am done.

41.play: MERCUTIO: Tut, dun's the mouse, the constable's own word:

42.play: If thou art dun, we'll draw thee from the mire

43.play: Of this sir-reverence love, wherein thou stick'st

44.play: Up to the ears. Come, we burn daylight, ho!

46.play: MERCUTIO: I mean, sir, in delay

47.play: We waste our lights in vain, like lamps by day.

48.play: Take our good meaning, for our judgment sits

49.play: Five times in that ere once in our five wits.

50.play: ROMEO: And we mean well in going to this mask;

53.play: ROMEO: I dream'd a dream to-night.

57.play: ROMEO: In bed asleep, while they do dream things true.

58.play: MERCUTIO: O, then, I see Queen Mab hath been with you.

59.play: She is the fairies' midwife, and she comes

60.play: In shape no bigger than an agate-stone

61.play: On the fore-finger of an alderman,

62.play: Drawn with a team of little atomies

63.play: Athwart men's noses as they lie asleep;

64.play: Her wagon-spokes made of long spiders' legs,

65.play: The cover of the wings of grasshoppers,

66.play: The traces of the smallest spider's web,

67.play: The collars of the moonshine's watery beams,

68.play: Her whip of cricket's bone, the lash of film,

69.play: Her wagoner a small grey-coated gnat,

70.play: Not so big as a round little worm

71.play: Prick'd from the lazy finger of a maid;

72.play: Her chariot is an empty hazel-nut

73.play: Made by the joiner squirrel or old grub,

74.play: Time out o' mind the fairies' coachmakers.

75.play: And in this state she gallops night by night

76.play: Through lovers' brains, and then they dream of love;

77.play: O'er courtiers' knees, that dream on court'sies straight,

78.play: O'er lawyers' fingers, who straight dream on fees,

79.play: O'er ladies ' lips, who straight on kisses dream,

80.play: Which oft the angry Mab with blisters plagues,

81.play: Because their breaths with sweetmeats tainted are:

82.play: Sometime she gallops o'er a courtier's nose,

83.play: And then dreams he of smelling out a suit;

84.play: And sometime comes she with a tithe-pig's tail

85.play: Tickling a parson's nose as a' lies asleep,

86.play: Then dreams, he of another benefice:

87.play: Sometime she driveth o'er a soldier's neck,

88.play: And then dreams he of cutting foreign throats,

90.play: Of healths five-fathom deep; and then anon

91.play: Drums in his ear, at which he starts and wakes,

92.play: And being thus frighted swears a prayer or two

93.play: And sleeps again. This is that very Mab

94.play: That plats the manes of horses in the night,

95.play: And bakes the elflocks in foul sluttish hairs,

96.play: Which once untangled, much misfortune bodes:

97.play: This is the hag, when maids lie on their backs,

98.play: That presses them and learns them first to bear,

99.play: Making them women of good carriage:

103.play: MERCUTIO: True, I talk of dreams,

104.play: Which are the children of an idle brain,

105.play: Begot of nothing but vain fantasy,

106.play: Which is as thin of substance as the air

107.play: And more inconstant than the wind, who wooes

108.play: Even now the frozen bosom of the north,

13.play: We sucking on her natural bosom find,

185.play: Of fair demesnes, youthful, and nobly train'd,

186.play: Stuff'd, as they say, with honourable parts,

187.play: Proportion'd as one's thought would wish a man;

188.play: And then to have a wretched puling fool,

189.play: A whining mammet, in her fortune's tender,

190.play: To answer 'I'll not wed; I cannot love,

191.play: I am too young; I pray you, pardon me.'

192.play: But, as you will not wed, I'll pardon you:

193.play: Graze where you will you shall not house with me:

194.play: Look to't, think on't, I do not use to jest.

195.play: Thursday is near; lay hand on heart, advise:

196.play: An you be mine, I'll give you to my friend;

197.play: And you be not, hang, beg, starve, die in

199.play: For, by my soul, I'll ne'er acknowledge thee,

200.play: Nor what is mine shall never do thee good:

201.play: Trust to't, bethink you; I'll not be forsworn.

202.play: JULIET: Is there no pity sitting in the clouds,

203.play: That sees into the bottom of my grief?

204.play: O, sweet my mother, cast me not away!

205.play: Delay this marriage for a month, a week;

206.play: Or, if you do not, make the bridal bed

207.play: In that dim monument where Tybalt lies.

208.play: LADY CAPULET: Talk not to me, for I'll not speak a word:

209.play: Do as thou wilt, for I have done with thee.

210.play: JULIET: O God!--O nurse, how shall this be prevented?

211.play: My husband is on earth, my faith in heaven;

212.play: How shall that faith return again to earth,

213.play: Unless that husband send it me from heaven

214.play: By leaving earth? comfort me, counsel me.

215.play: Alack, alack, that heaven should practise stratagems

216.play: Upon so soft a subject as myself!

217.play: What say'st thou? hast thou not a word of joy?

221.play: Well, thou hast comforted me marvellous much.

222.play: Go in: and tell my lady I am gone,

223.play: Having displeased my father, to Laurence' cell,

224.play: To make confession and to be absolved.

226.play: JULIET: Ancient damnation! O most wicked fiend!

227.play: Is it more sin to wish me thus forsworn,

228.play: Or to dispraise my lord with that same tongue

229.play: Which she hath praised him with above compare

230.play: So many thousand times? Go, counsellor;

231.play: Thou and my bosom henceforth shall be twain.

232.play: I'll to the friar, to know his remedy:

233.play: If all else fail, myself have power to die.

2.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: On Thursday, sir? the time is very short.

3.play: PARIS: My father Capulet will have it so;

4.play: And I am nothing slow to slack his haste.

5.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: You say you do not know the lady's mind:

6.play: Uneven is the course, I like it not.

7.play: PARIS: Immoderately she weeps for Tybalt's death,

8.play: And therefore have I little talk'd of love;

9.play: For Venus smiles not in a house of tears.

10.play: Now, sir, her father counts it dangerous

11.play: That she doth give her sorrow so much sway,

12.play: And in his wisdom hastes our marriage,

13.play: To stop the inundation of her tears;

14.play: Which, too much minded by herself alone,

15.play: May be put from her by society:

16.play: Now do you know the reason of this haste.

17.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: [Aside]  I would I knew not why it should be slow'd.

18.play: Look, sir, here comes the lady towards my cell.

19.play: PARIS: Happily met, my lady and my wife!

20.play: JULIET: That may be, sir, when I may be a wife.

21.play: PARIS: That may be must be, love, on Thursday next.

22.play: JULIET: What must be shall be.

23.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: That's a certain text.

24.play: PARIS: Come you to make confession to this father?

25.play: JULIET: To answer that, I should confess to you.

26.play: PARIS: Do not deny to him that you love me.

27.play: JULIET: I will confess to you that I love him.

28.play: PARIS: So will ye, I am sure, that you love me.

29.play: JULIET: If I do so, it will be of more price,

30.play: Being spoke behind your back, than to your face.

31.play: PARIS: Poor soul, thy face is much abused with tears.

32.play: JULIET: The tears have got small victory by that;

33.play: For it was bad enough before their spite.

34.play: PARIS: Thou wrong'st it, more than tears, with that report.

35.play: JULIET: That is no slander, sir, which is a truth;

36.play: And what I spake, I spake it to my face.

37.play: PARIS: Thy face is mine, and thou hast slander'd it.

38.play: JULIET: It may be so, for it is not mine own.

39.play: Are you at leisure, holy father, now;

40.play: Or shall I come to you at evening mass?

41.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: My leisure serves me, pensive daughter, now.

42.play: My lord, we must entreat the time alone.

43.play: PARIS: God shield I should disturb devotion!

44.play: Juliet, on Thursday early will I rouse ye:

45.play: Till then, adieu; and keep this holy kiss.

46.play: JULIET: O shut the door! and when thou hast done so,

47.play: Come weep with me; past hope, past cure, past help!

48.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: Ah, Juliet, I already know thy grief;

49.play: It strains me past the compass of my wits:

50.play: I hear thou must, and nothing may prorogue it,

51.play: On Thursday next be married to this county.

52.play: JULIET: Tell me not, friar, that thou hear'st of this,

53.play: Unless thou tell me how I may prevent it:

54.play: If, in thy wisdom, thou canst give no help,

55.play: Do thou but call my resolution wise,

56.play: And with this knife I'll help it presently.

57.play: God join'd my heart and Romeo's, thou our hands;

58.play: And ere this hand, by thee to Romeo seal'd,

59.play: Shall be the label to another deed,

60.play: Or my true heart with treacherous revolt

61.play: Turn to another, this shall slay them both:

62.play: Therefore, out of thy long-experienced time,

63.play: Give me some present counsel, or, behold,

64.play: 'Twixt my extremes and me this bloody knife

65.play: Shall play the umpire, arbitrating that

66.play: Which the commission of thy years and art

67.play: Could to no issue of true honour bring.

68.play: Be not so long to speak; I long to die,

69.play: If what thou speak'st speak not of remedy.

70.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: Hold, daughter: I do spy a kind of hope,

71.play: Which craves as desperate an execution.

72.play: As that is desperate which we would prevent.

73.play: If, rather than to marry County Paris,

74.play: Thou hast the strength of will to slay thyself,

75.play: Then is it likely thou wilt undertake

76.play: A thing like death to chide away this shame,

77.play: That copest with death himself to scape from it:

78.play: And, if thou darest, I'll give thee remedy.

79.play: JULIET: O, bid me leap, rather than marry Paris,

80.play: From off the battlements of yonder tower;

81.play: Or walk in thievish ways; or bid me lurk

82.play: Where serpents are; chain me with roaring bears;

83.play: Or shut me nightly in a charnel-house,

84.play: O'er-cover'd quite with dead men's rattling bones,

85.play: With reeky shanks and yellow chapless skulls;

86.play: Or bid me go into a new-made grave

87.play: And hide me with a dead man in his shroud;

88.play: Things that, to hear them told, have made me tremble;

89.play: And I will do it without fear or doubt,

90.play: To live an unstain'd wife to my sweet love.

91.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: Hold, then; go home, be merry, give consent

92.play: To marry Paris: Wednesday is to-morrow:

93.play: To-morrow night look that thou lie alone;

94.play: Let not thy nurse lie with thee in thy chamber:

95.play: Take thou this vial, being then in bed,

96.play: And this distilled liquor drink thou off;

97.play: When presently through all thy veins shall run

98.play: A cold and drowsy humour, for no pulse

99.play: Shall keep his native progress, but surcease:

100.play: No warmth, no breath, shall testify thou livest;

101.play: The roses in thy lips and cheeks shall fade

102.play: To paly ashes, thy eyes' windows fall,

103.play: Like death, when he shuts up the day of life;

104.play: Each part, deprived of supple government,

105.play: Shall, stiff and stark and cold, appear like death:

106.play: And in this borrow'd likeness of shrunk death

107.play: Thou shalt continue two and forty hours,

108.play: And then awake as from a pleasant sleep.

109.play: Now, when the bridegroom in the morning comes

110.play: To rouse thee from thy bed, there art thou dead:

111.play: Then, as the manner of our country is,

112.play: In thy best robes uncover'd on the bier

113.play: Thou shalt be borne to that same ancient vault

114.play: Where all the kindred of the Capulets lie.

115.play: In the mean time, against thou shalt awake,

116.play: Shall Romeo by my letters know our drift,

117.play: And hither shall he come: and he and I

118.play: Will watch thy waking, and that very night

119.play: Shall Romeo bear thee hence to Mantua.

120.play: And this shall free thee from this present shame;

121.play: If no inconstant toy, nor womanish fear,

122.play: Abate thy valour in the acting it.

123.play: JULIET: Give me, give me! O, tell not me of fear!

124.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: Hold; get you gone, be strong and prosperous

125.play: In this resolve: I'll send a friar with speed

126.play: To Mantua, with my letters to thy lord.

127.play: JULIET: Love give me strength! and strength shall help afford.

1.play: Enter CAPULET, LADY  CAPULET, Nurse, and two Servingmen

2.play: CAPULET: So many guests invite as here are writ.

3.play: Sirrah, go hire me twenty cunning cooks.

4.play: How canst thou try them so?

5.play: Go, be gone.

6.play: We shall be much unfurnished for this time.

7.play: What, is my daughter gone to Friar Laurence?

8.play: Well, he may chance to do some good on her:

9.play: A peevish self-will'd harlotry it is.

10.play: Enter JULIET

11.play: CAPULET: How now, my headstrong! where have you been gadding?

12.play: JULIET: Where I have learn'd me to repent the sin

13.play: Of disobedient opposition

14.play: To you and your behests, and am enjoin'd

15.play: By holy Laurence to fall prostrate here,

16.play: And beg your pardon: pardon, I beseech you!

17.play: Henceforward I am ever ruled by you.

18.play: CAPULET: Send for the county; go tell him of this:

19.play: I'll have this knot knit up to-morrow morning.

20.play: JULIET: I met the youthful lord at Laurence' cell;

21.play: And gave him what becomed love I might,

22.play: Not step o'er the bounds of modesty.

23.play: CAPULET: Why, I am glad on't; this is well: stand up:

24.play: This is as't should be. Let me see the county;

25.play: Ay, marry, go, I say, and fetch him hither.

26.play: Now, afore God! this reverend holy friar,

27.play: Our whole city is much bound to him.

28.play: JULIET: Nurse, will you go with me into my closet,

29.play: To help me sort such needful ornaments

30.play: As you think fit to furnish me to-morrow?

31.play: LADY CAPULET: No, not till Thursday; there is time enough.

32.play: CAPULET: Go, nurse, go with her: we'll to church to-morrow.

33.play: LADY  CAPULET: We shall be short in our provision:

34.play: 'Tis now near night.

35.play: CAPULET: Tush, I will stir about,

36.play: And all things shall be well, I warrant thee, wife:

37.play: Go thou to Juliet, help to deck up her;

38.play: I'll not to bed to-night; let me alone;

39.play: I'll play the housewife for this once. What, ho!

40.play: They are all forth. Well, I will walk myself

41.play: To County Paris, to prepare him up

42.play: Against to-morrow: my heart is wondrous light,

43.play: Since this same wayward girl is so reclaim'd.

2.play: JULIET: Ay, those attires are best: but, gentle nurse,

3.play: I pray thee, leave me to my self to-night,

4.play: For I have need of many orisons

5.play: To move the heavens to smile upon my state,

6.play: Which, well thou know'st, is cross, and full of sin.

7.play: LADY CAPULET: What, are you busy, ho? need you my help?

8.play: JULIET: No, madam; we have cull'd such necessaries

9.play: As are behoveful for our state to-morrow:

10.play: So please you, let me now be left alone,

11.play: And let the nurse this night sit up with you;

12.play: For, I am sure, you have your hands full all,

13.play: In this so sudden business.

14.play: LADY CAPULET: Good night:

15.play: Get thee to bed, and rest; for thou hast need.

16.play: JULIET: Farewell! God knows when we shall meet again.

17.play: I have a faint cold fear thrills through my veins,

18.play: That almost freezes up the heat of life:

19.play: I'll call them back again to comfort me:

20.play: Nurse! What should she do here?

21.play: My dismal scene I needs must act alone.

22.play: Come, vial.

23.play: What if this mixture do not work at all?

24.play: Shall I be married then to-morrow morning?

25.play: No, no: this shall forbid it: lie thou there.

26.play: What if it be a poison, which the friar

27.play: Subtly hath minister'd to have me dead,

28.play: Lest in this marriage he should be dishonour'd,

29.play: Because he married me before to Romeo?

30.play: I fear it is: and yet, methinks, it should not,

31.play: For he hath still been tried a holy man.

32.play: How if, when I am laid into the tomb,

33.play: I wake before the time that Romeo

34.play: Come to redeem me? there's a fearful point!

35.play: Shall I not, then, be stifled in the vault,

36.play: To whose foul mouth no healthsome air breathes in,

37.play: And there die strangled ere my Romeo comes?

38.play: Or, if I live, is it not very like,

39.play: The horrible conceit of death and night,

40.play: Together with the terror of the place,--

41.play: As in a vault, an ancient receptacle,

42.play: Where, for these many hundred years, the bones

43.play: Of all my buried ancestors are packed:

44.play: Where bloody Tybalt, yet but green in earth,

45.play: Lies festering in his shroud; where, as they say,

46.play: At some hours in the night spirits resort;--

47.play: Alack, alack, is it not like that I,

48.play: So early waking, what with loathsome smells,

49.play: And shrieks like mandrakes' torn out of the earth,

50.play: That living mortals, hearing them, run mad:--

51.play: O, if I wake, shall I not be distraught,

52.play: Environed with all these hideous fears?

53.play: And madly play with my forefather's joints?

54.play: And pluck the mangled Tybalt from his shroud?

55.play: And, in this rage, with some great kinsman's bone,

56.play: As with a club, dash out my desperate brains?

57.play: O, look! methinks I see my cousin's ghost

58.play: Seeking out Romeo, that did spit his body

59.play: Upon a rapier's point: stay, Tybalt, stay!

60.play: Romeo, I come! this do I drink to thee.

2.play: LADY CAPULET: Hold, take these keys, and fetch more spices, nurse.

3.play: Enter CAPULET

4.play: CAPULET: Come, stir, stir, stir! the second cock hath crow'd,

5.play: The curfew-bell hath rung, 'tis three o'clock:

6.play: Look to the baked meats, good Angelica:

7.play: Spare not for the cost.

8.play: No, not a whit: what! I have watch'd ere now

9.play: All night for lesser cause, and ne'er been sick.

10.play: LADY CAPULET: Ay, you have been a mouse-hunt in your time;

11.play: But I will watch you from such watching now.

12.play: CAPULET: A jealous hood, a jealous hood!

13.play: Now, fellow,

14.play: What's there?

15.play: Make haste, make haste.

16.play: Sirrah, fetch drier logs:

17.play: Call Peter, he will show thee where they are.

18.play: Exit

19.play: CAPULET: Mass, and well said; a merry whoreson, ha!

20.play: Thou shalt be logger-head. Good faith, 'tis day:

21.play: The county will be here with music straight,

22.play: For so he said he would: I hear him near.

23.play: Nurse! Wife! What, ho! What, nurse, I say!

24.play: Go waken Juliet, go and trim her up;

25.play: I'll go and chat with Paris: hie, make haste,

26.play: Make haste; the bridegroom he is come already:

27.play: Make haste, I say.

2.play: LADY CAPULET: What noise is here?

3.play: What is the matter?

4.play: O me, O me! My child, my only life,

5.play: Revive, look up, or I will die with thee!

6.play: Help, help! Call help.

7.play: CAPULET: For shame, bring Juliet forth; her lord is come.

8.play: LADY CAPULET: Alack the day, she's dead, she's dead, she's dead!

9.play: CAPULET: Ha! let me see her: out, alas! she's cold:

10.play: Her blood is settled, and her joints are stiff;

11.play: Life and these lips have long been separated:

12.play: Death lies on her like an untimely frost

13.play: Upon the sweetest flower of all the field.

14.play: LADY CAPULET: O woful time!

15.play: CAPULET: Death, that hath ta'en her hence to make me wail,

16.play: Ties up my tongue, and will not let me speak.

17.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: Come, is the bride ready to go to church?

18.play: CAPULET: Ready to go, but never to return.

19.play: O son! the night before thy wedding-day

20.play: Hath Death lain with thy wife. There she lies,

21.play: Flower as she was, deflowered by him.

22.play: Death is my son-in-law, Death is my heir;

23.play: My daughter he hath wedded: I will die,

24.play: And leave him all; life, living, all is Death's.

25.play: PARIS: Have I thought long to see this morning's face,

26.play: And doth it give me such a sight as this?

27.play: LADY CAPULET: Accursed, unhappy, wretched, hateful day!

28.play: Most miserable hour that e'er time saw

29.play: In lasting labour of his pilgrimage!

30.play: But one, poor one, one poor and loving child,

31.play: But one thing to rejoice and solace in,

32.play: And cruel death hath catch'd it from my sight!

33.play: PARIS: Beguiled, divorced, wronged, spited, slain!

34.play: Most detestable death, by thee beguil'd,

35.play: By cruel cruel thee quite overthrown!

36.play: O love! O life! not life, but love in death!

37.play: CAPULET: Despised, distressed, hated, martyr'd, kill'd!

38.play: Uncomfortable time, why camest thou now

39.play: To murder, murder our solemnity?

40.play: O child! O child! my soul, and not my child!

41.play: Dead art thou! Alack! my child is dead;

42.play: And with my child my joys are buried.

43.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: Peace, ho, for shame! confusion's cure lives not

44.play: In these confusions. Heaven and yourself

45.play: Had part in this fair maid; now heaven hath all,

46.play: And all the better is it for the maid:

47.play: Your part in her you could not keep from death,

48.play: But heaven keeps his part in eternal life.

49.play: The most you sought was her promotion;

50.play: For 'twas your heaven she should be advanced:

51.play: And weep ye now, seeing she is advanced

52.play: Above the clouds, as high as heaven itself?

53.play: O, in this love, you love your child so ill,

54.play: That you run mad, seeing that she is well:

55.play: She's not well married that lives married long;

56.play: But she's best married that dies married young.

57.play: Dry up your tears, and stick your rosemary

58.play: On this fair corse; and, as the custom is,

59.play: In all her best array bear her to church:

60.play: For though fond nature bids us an lament,

61.play: Yet nature's tears are reason's merriment.

62.play: CAPULET: All things that we ordained festival,

63.play: Turn from their office to black funeral;

65.play: Our wedding cheer to a sad burial feast,

66.play: Our solemn hymns to sullen dirges change,

67.play: Our bridal flowers serve for a buried corse,

68.play: And all things change them to the contrary.

69.play: FRIAR LAURENCE: Sir, go you in; and, madam, go with him;

70.play: And go, Sir Paris; every one prepare

71.play: To follow this fair corse unto her grave:

72.play: The heavens do lour upon you for some ill;

73.play: Move them no more by crossing their high will.

76.play: PETER: Musicians, O, musicians, 'Heart's ease, Heart's

77.play: ease:' O, an you will have me live, play 'Heart's ease.'

78.play: O, musicians, because my heart itself plays 'My

79.play: heart is full of woe:' O, play me some merry dump,

82.play: I will then give it you soundly.

83.play: No money, on my faith, but the gleek;

84.play: I will give you the minstrel.

85.play: Then will I lay the serving-creature's dagger on

86.play: your pate. I will carry no crotchets: I'll re you,

87.play: I'll fa you; do you note me?

88.play: Then have at you with my wit! I will dry-beat you

89.play: with an iron wit, and put up my iron dagger. Answer

91.play: 'When griping grief the heart doth wound,

92.play: And doleful dumps the mind oppress,

93.play: Then music with her silver sound'--

94.play: why 'silver sound'? why 'music with her silver

95.play: sound'? What say you, Simon Catling?

96.play: Pretty! What say you, Hugh Rebeck?

97.play: Pretty too! What say you, James Soundpost?

98.play: O, I cry you mercy; you are the singer: I will say

99.play: for you. It is 'music with her silver sound,'

100.play: because musicians have no gold for sounding:

102.play: With speedy help doth lend redress.'

2.play: ROMEO: If I may trust the flattering truth of sleep,

3.play: My dreams presage some joyful news at hand:

4.play: My bosom's lord sits lightly in his throne;

5.play: And all this day an unaccustom'd spirit

6.play: Lifts me above the ground with cheerful thoughts.

7.play: I dreamt my lady came and found me dead--

8.play: Strange dream, that gives a dead man leave

9.play: to think!--

10.play: And breathed such life with kisses in my lips,

11.play: That I revived, and was an emperor.

12.play: Ah me! how sweet is love itself possess'd,

13.play: When but love's shadows are so rich in joy!

14.play: News from Verona!--How now, Balthasar!

15.play: Dost thou not bring me letters from the friar?

16.play: How doth my lady? Is my father well?

17.play: How fares my Juliet? that I ask again;

18.play: For nothing can be ill, if she be well.

19.play: BALTHASAR: Then she is well, and nothing can be ill:

20.play: Her body sleeps in Capel's monument,

21.play: And her immortal part with angels lives.

22.play: I saw her laid low in her kindred's vault,

23.play: And presently took post to tell it you:

24.play: O, pardon me for bringing these ill news,

25.play: Since you did leave it for my office, sir.

26.play: ROMEO: Is it even so? then I defy you, stars!

27.play: Thou know'st my lodging: get me ink and paper,

28.play: And hire post-horses; I will hence to-night.

29.play: BALTHASAR: I do beseech you, sir, have patience:

30.play: Your looks are pale and wild, and do import

31.play: Some misadventure.

32.play: ROMEO: Tush, thou art deceived:

33.play: Leave me, and do the thing I bid thee do.

34.play: Hast thou no letters to me from the friar?

35.play: BALTHASAR: No, my good lord.

36.play: ROMEO: No matter: get thee gone,

37.play: And hire those horses; I'll be with thee straight.

38.play: Well, Juliet, I will lie with thee to-night.

39.play: Let's see for means: O mischief, thou art swift

40.play: To enter in the thoughts of desperate men!

41.play: I do remember an apothecary,--

42.play: And hereabouts he dwells,--which late I noted

43.play: In tatter'd weeds, with overwhelming brows,

44.play: Culling of simples; meagre were his looks,

45.play: Sharp misery had worn him to the bones:

46.play: And in his needy shop a tortoise hung,

47.play: An alligator stuff'd, and other skins

48.play: Of ill-shaped fishes; and about his shelves

49.play: A beggarly account of empty boxes,

50.play: Green earthen pots, bladders and musty seeds,

51.play: Remnants of packthread and old cakes of roses,

52.play: Were thinly scatter'd, to make up a show.

53.play: Noting this penury, to myself I said

54.play: 'An if a man did need a poison now,

55.play: Whose sale is present death in Mantua,

56.play: Here lives a caitiff wretch would sell it him.'

57.play: O, this same thought did but forerun my need;

58.play: And this same needy man must sell it me.

59.play: As I remember, this should be the house.

60.play: Being holiday, the beggar's shop is shut.

62.play: Come hither, man. I see that thou art poor:

63.play: Hold, there is forty ducats: let me have

64.play: A dram of poison, such soon-speeding gear

65.play: As will disperse itself through all the veins

66.play: That the life-weary taker may fall dead

67.play: And that the trunk may be discharged of breath

68.play: As violently as hasty powder fired

69.play: Doth hurry from the fatal cannon's womb.

2.play: FRIAR JOHN: Holy Franciscan friar! brother, ho!

7.play: One of our order, to associate me,

8.play: Here in this city visiting the sick,

9.play: And finding him, the searchers of the town,

10.play: Suspecting that we both were in a house

17.play: So fearful were they of infection.

19.play: The letter was not nice but full of charge

20.play: Of dear import, and the neglecting it

1.play: Enter PARIS, and his Page bearing flowers and a torch

2.play: PARIS: Give me thy torch, boy: hence, and stand aloof:

3.play: Yet put it out, for I would not be seen.

4.play: Under yond yew-trees lay thee all along,

5.play: Holding thine ear close to the hollow ground;

6.play: So shall no foot upon the churchyard tread,

7.play: Being loose, unfirm, with digging up of graves,

11.play: PAGE: [Aside]  I am almost afraid to stand alone

12.play: Here in the churchyard; yet I will adventure.

13.play: PARIS: Sweet flower, with flowers thy bridal bed I strew,--

14.play: O woe! thy canopy is dust and stones;--

16.play: Or, wanting that, with tears distill'd by moans:

22.play: What with a torch! muffle me, night, awhile.

30.play: Is partly to behold my lady's face;

31.play: But chiefly to take thence from her dead finger

32.play: A precious ring, a ring that I must use

33.play: In dear employment: therefore hence, be gone:

42.play: ROMEO: So shalt thou show me friendship. Take thou that:
